OVER-DONE/INERT: the Household weekly-plan grid (household-s01) with the 'All' pills is the most interaction-rich surface in the group and is a genuine micro-delight opportunity — toggling a cell between solo/everyone is exactly where a 'selection' haptic + a colour fill animation would make it feel tactile. Currently reads as a static grid.

WEB ANALOG NOTE: web settings have no haptics; for each win-moment above (Health connect, save commit, grid toggle) the web equivalent should mirror with a 200ms confirming micro-animation + a success-green colour pulse so the win lands on both platforms (per the mobile-decisions-apply-to-web rule). The web settings sub-pages were not in this capture set — flag as uncovered for the synthesis.

No motion personality is observable across the static captures — sub-page entry, card mount, and toggle state changes appear to use no shared transition. The group reads as instant-swap navigation rather than a product with a motion signature. This is a whole-app direction item, not settings-specific.

topIssues.title: Notifications sub-page crashes — renders an error boundary, not the screen
topIssues.surface: Notifications (notifications-s00 redbox + notifications-s01 'Something went wrong' boundary)
topIssues.why: VERIFIED AGAINST HEAD (cda3c92, capture 08:41 > commit 08:27 — not a stale-bundle phantom). The redbox cites notifications.tsx:226 and the channel name 'mobile:notif:e9f85055-...' matches source exactly. Root cause: the realtime effect depends on [userId, loadInbox]; loadInbox is a useCallback whose identity changes, re-running the effect. On re-run, supabase.channel('mobile:notif:${userId}') reuses the already-subscribed channel instance and the second .on('postgres_changes') is added after subscribe() — Supabase throws 'cannot add postgres_changes callbacks after subscribe()'. The entire Notifications surface (a settings sub-page reachable from the hub and More) is dead. This is a hard cohesion break: one tab of the wall is a stack trace.
topIssues.fix: Stabilise the realtime subscription: either remove loadInbox from the effect deps and call the latest via a ref, or build+subscribe the channel exactly once per userId (deps [userId] only) so .on() is never chained onto an already-subscribed channel. Owner: executor (bug) with sync-enforcer checking the web notifications inbox for the same pattern. Ships behind no flag — it's a crash fix, not a visual change.

topIssues.title: Depth model is flat across the entire group — Elevation.card is a zeroed no-op
topIssues.surface: Every card on Settings hub, Profile, Targets, Household, Health Sync (settings-s00/s01, household-s00, health-sync-s00)
topIssues.why: One token (Elevation.card in theme.ts) is set to shadowOpacity:0/elevation:0, so all settings cards are flat fills separated from the paper background only by a hairline edge. This is the dominant 'Generic not Premium' read for the group and it's a single root, not 15 symptoms. Leading settings surfaces (Things, Copilot Money, Oura) use one soft ~6-8% shadow with no hairline borders; Suppr's own Targets ring card proves the team can do depth — it just isn't applied.
topIssues.fix: Introduce a single soft-elevation token (e.g. shadowOpacity ~0.06-0.08, radius ~12, offset y4, no border) and apply it to the card primitive used across the group; delete hairline borders that were standing in for shadow. Owner: ui-product-designer to define the elevation, design-system-enforcer to encode it as the one card token. Ship behind isFeatureEnabled flag.

topIssues.title: Dev marker leaks into a user-facing Settings row
topIssues.surface: Settings hub Build section (settings-s03/s04, more-s02): 'v1.0.7 · build ? · MARKER F50-2026-04-22'
topIssues.why: A user-facing surface shows a literal '?' for build number and an internal dev marker string ('MARKER F50-2026-04-22'). This is exactly the dev-chrome-bleeds-into-frame failure the pixel-audit memory warns about. On a settings screen — the one place users check 'what version am I on' — it reads as unfinished/broken and undercuts the otherwise-calm signature.
topIssues.fix: Resolve the build number at build time (real number, not '?') and strip the MARKER token from any release build's version string. Owner: executor. Pure string fix, no flag needed.

topIssues.title: Hardcoded Tailwind red breaks the token system
topIssues.why: The only hardcoded hex in the entire group, and it's a specifically-banned Tailwind hex per project context. Means the error state won't follow theme/dark-mode token shifts and drifts from the semantic --destructive used elsewhere. Low blast radius (one screen, one state) but it's a clean root fix that keeps the palette census at zero hardcoded hexes for the group.
topIssues.fix: Replace '#f87171' with the semantic destructive token (colors.destructive / Accent equivalent). Owner: design-system-enforcer / executor. No flag.

topIssues.title: Header + section-label languages drift within one group
topIssues.surface: Nutrition Sources one-off header (more-s00: '< Back' + small-caps 'INFO' eyebrow) vs chevron+bold-title elsewhere; Household ALL-CAPS labels (household-s00) vs Title Case on hub/More
topIssues.why: Two header patterns and two section-label-case systems inside the same settings group make it read as assembled by different people. Nutrition Sources in particular uses a header that matches no other Suppr screen. This is the classic 'pile of screens that share a logo' tell — small individually, corrosive in aggregate.
topIssues.fix: Standardise on one header (left chevron + bold screen title) and one section-label style (Title Case grey caption — the dominant, calmer choice). Retire the 'INFO' eyebrow + '< Back' text on Nutrition Sources; convert Household's ALL-CAPS labels to Title Case. Owner: ui-product-designer to spec, executor to apply. Ship behind flag (copy/layout meaning).

topIssues.title: Win-moments are silent — no haptic/colour celebration on connect or save
topIssues.surface: Health Sync connect (health-sync-s00/s01), Household/Profile save commits (household-s00, profile-s01), Household weekly-plan grid toggles (household-s01)
topIssues.why: The most rewarding settings actions — connecting Apple Health (data comes alive), saving targets/household, toggling shared meals — fire as silent default-iOS toggle flips with no success haptic, no green-confirm animation, and stock grey switches with no brand tint. The success-green and PressableScale haptic primitives already exist in the system; they just aren't wired to where the wins happen. This is the group's biggest engagement/delight gap.
topIssues.fix: Wire PressableScale haptic='success' + animate the row to the green 'Connected' state on Health connect; haptic='confirm' on every save commit; haptic='selection' + colour-fill on weekly-plan cell toggles. Replace the default switch with a periwinkle-tinted custom switch. Mirror each as a 200ms confirm animation + green pulse on web. Owner: ui-product-designer + sync-enforcer for web parity. Ship behind flag.

reviews.signature: Partial. With the logo cropped, the FASTING and WHAT'S-NEW screens read unmistakably as Suppr: warm cream backgrounds (#fbfaf6/#f5f3ec), warm-ink text tokens (#5b554b / #8a8377), the #588ce4 periwinkle primary, the success-green (#56A775) celebratory state, calm adult copy ("Fast when you're ready", "A fasting window is just a way to structure when you eat"). That is a real, defensible point of view and it is carried consistently across fasting + paywall + what's-new. The signature BREAKS hard on one surface: the ERROR BOUNDARY is a different product entirely — a cool slate-on-near-black world (#94a3b8 / #64748b body text on #0a0a0f) with no warm tone, no token system, a system-default-looking blue button. It is the only dark screen in an otherwise light, warm group, and it is built from hardcoded hexes that exist nowhere else in the palette. So: a strong identity on the happy-path surfaces, abandoned on the one screen a user sees at their most fragile moment.
palette: GROUNDED PIXEL FACT: all three primary CTAs sample to EXACTLY #588ce4 (error-boundary 'Try again', fasting 'Start Fast', paywall 'Open App Store') — the canonical Accent.primary 8-slot-lock blue. No blue-drift in this group; that is a strength, hold it up as the bar.

ROOT CAUSE — error boundary is off-palette (STATE-error-boundary.png, verified RootErrorBoundary.tsx): every text colour is a hardcoded COOL-SLATE hex (#e4e4e8 title, #94a3b8 body, #64748b footer) on a hardcoded #0a0a0f. The rest of the app uses WARM-INK tokens (#5b554b textSecondary, #8a8377 textTertiary). The error screen is literally a different colour temperature from every other Suppr surface — it reads as a stock RN template, not Suppr.

CONTRAST FAIL (STATE-error-boundary.png): the 'Still not working? Force-quit…' footer #64748b on #0a0a0f computes to 4.15:1 — below the 4.5:1 AA body threshold. The reassurance line a panicking user most needs is the hardest to read.

EMOTIONAL-PULL GAP (paywall, dl-paywall.png): the 'SAVE 37%' badge is the only warm/positive colour pull on the screen and it is a small muted-green pill in the top-right corner. The price (£59.99/year) sits in plain ink with no emotional framing. A money-app at the world bar (Copilot, MacroFactor) makes the saving the hero — e.g. the effective '/mo' number in success-green at display size — so the value, not the sticker price, is what the eye lands on first.

EMOTIONAL-PULL GAP (fasting complete state, code-verified): on fast completion the ring + 'Complete Fast' button flip to success-green (#56A775) — correct and on-brand — but the rest of the screen stays neutral. The single biggest win-moment in this feature gets one colour change and no warm accent or gradient to mark the achievement.
consistency: RADIUS DRIFT — three different card corner radii across four surfaces in ONE group (all HEAD-verified): paywall card borderRadius:18 (paywall.tsx:750), fasting + what's-new cards Radius.lg = 8 (fasting.tsx:239, whats-new.tsx:110), error-boundary button hardcoded borderRadius:12 (RootErrorBoundary.tsx:94). The radius scale only defines up to xl:12, so paywall's 18 is an off-token value with no home in the system.

DEPTH PRIMITIVE DRIFT — every card in the group uses borderWidth:1 + colors.border as its ONLY separation (fasting.tsx:240, whats-new.tsx:111, paywall.tsx:752 hairlineWidth). Zero shadow/elevation anywhere. This is the hairline-border-as-shadow tell repeated 4×; it is a single root primitive (the card surface) that needs one elevation model, not four flat outlines.

TOKEN-BYPASS — error boundary hardcodes 5 hexes + a radius instead of consuming the theme (Colors.dark.*, Radius.*). It is the one surface in the group that doesn't route through the token system, which is exactly why it drifts off-palette and off-radius.

LIGHT-VS-DARK SURFACE — fasting, paywall, what's-new all render light/warm (#fbfaf6, #f5f3ec, #ffffff cards); the error boundary is the lone #0a0a0f dark screen. The documented carve-out (mobile Today #0a0a0f) is about the TODAY surface tone, not a licence for an error screen to be the only dark surface in a light flow.

FASTING SAVE-CHIP vs PAYWALL SAVE-CHIP — both surface a savings idea but with different treatments: fasting uses plain bold text, paywall uses a pill 'SAVE 37%'. Minor, but the 'saving' concept should have one chip treatment product-wide.

NOTE — paywall defaults to annual (£59.99/year, the only 7-day-trial path; paywall.tsx:58) which appears to conflict with the project-context 'unified monthly default ENG-698'. Routing this to sync-enforcer as a parity/product question, NOT filing it as a design defect.
depth: FLAT-CARD CLASS (the root depth issue) — fasting cards (ix-60/61/62), what's-new cards (dl-whats-new), and paywall card (dl-paywall) are ALL flat fills bounded by a 1px hairline with no shadow. Tier: Generic. They look like a wireframe coloured in, not a crafted surface. Best-in-class (Things, Oura, Cron, MacroFactor 2026) use a single soft low-opacity drop shadow and NO border; Suppr is one generation behind on its card material across this whole group.

ERROR BOUNDARY (STATE-error-boundary.png) — Tier: Cheap. Centered text + a single rounded button floating on flat near-black, cool-slate type, no Suppr mark, no warmth, no illustration or texture. It is indistinguishable from a default React error screen. This is the lowest-craft surface in the group and it is the one shown at the user's most anxious moment.

PAYWALL (dl-paywall.png) — Tier: Good, the best-built surface here. The Pro card has a clear blue emphasis border, the trust pills ('Cancel anytime in App Store', '7-day refund, no email needed', 'Price never changes mid-trial') are a genuine premium move and a real differentiator — keep them. But it still sits on the flat-hairline card material, so it stops short of Premium. A soft shadow + the value reframed as the hero would push it over.

FASTING IDLE ICON HALO (ix-60) — the stopwatch sits in a soft #f1f6fd blue circle; this is the ONE bit of crafted depth/tint in the group and it's nice. It proves the team can do tactility — it just isn't applied to the cards.

WHAT'S-NEW (dl-whats-new) — Tier: Generic. A plain bulleted list inside a flat outlined card. Functional, but it's the changelog of a product whose changelog brags about 'haptic confirmation' and 'anticipation beats' — the screen itself has none of that polish, which undercuts the message.
delight: FASTING is the delight high-point and it's genuinely good (code-verified, fasting.tsx): haptics.select() on Start, Haptics.notificationAsync(Warning) on a tap-to-end mis-tap, Haptics.notificationAsync(Success) on a real end, ring + button flip to success-green on completion. This is thoughtful, weight-appropriate haptic design — hold it up as the in-house bar for the rest of the app.

MISSING WIN-MOMENT (fasting complete) — completing a fast fires a Success haptic + green flip but NO motion celebration (no ring fill flourish, no confetti, no number count-up). The captured ix-62 even shows a degenerate '0h 0m' recent fast with zero acknowledgement. Reinforces the streak/habit loop — at the world bar (Oura/Whoop close-out) this moment gets a short, earned motion beat. Add a 1-shot ring-completion animation + count-up of the achieved duration.

PAYWALL delight is present and correct (code-verified): Haptics.selectionAsync() on plan tap, Haptics.notificationAsync(Success) on a completed purchase. Good. Opportunity: the purchase-success moment has a haptic but (from source) no visual celebration before the confirm Alert — the single highest-value conversion event in the app deserves a brief success motion, not just a buzz then a system alert.

WHAT'S-NEW is the dead surface (code-verified, whats-new.tsx) — useLayoutEffect only, NO haptics, NO entrance animation; the bullet list just snaps in. This is the changelog literally advertising new haptics and 'anticipation beats' while having none itself. Add a staggered fade/slide-in on the section list (reinforces 'this product is alive and improving' — the exact emotion a what's-new screen exists to create).

ERROR BOUNDARY — no haptic on appearance. A single light Haptics.notificationAsync(Error) when the boundary catches would give the user a felt signal that something happened and was handled, not just a silent screen-swap. Pair with a calmer, warmer visual so the felt signal isn't alarming.

WEB ANALOGS (mobile decisions must land on web): fasting-complete celebration → a CSS ring-fill + count-up on web; paywall purchase-success → a confirming micro-animation before redirect; what's-new entrance → the web changelog (web-desktop-whats-new.png is currently a plain static list too) gets the same staggered reveal. None of these win-moments currently have a web equivalent.

topIssues.title: Error boundary is off-palette, off-token, and off-brand — the lowest-craft surface in the product
topIssues.why: It is the only screen in this group built from hardcoded COOL-slate hexes (#94a3b8/#64748b/#e4e4e8) on #0a0a0f instead of the warm-ink token system, the only lone dark screen in a light/warm flow, and visually indistinguishable from a default RN error template. Plus its footer text (#64748b on #0a0a0f) is 4.15:1 — sub-AA. A user hits this at their most fragile moment and the product abandons its identity there.
topIssues.fix: Rebuild on the token system: warm Suppr surface (Colors.dark.* or, better, match the light warm-ink world the rest of the flow uses), warm-ink text tokens, Radius.* not hardcoded 12, the Suppr mark, and a body/footer colour that clears 4.5:1. Add a single Error haptic on catch. Ship behind isFeatureEnabled per CLAUDE.md.

topIssues.title: One card material — replace hairline-border-as-shadow with a single soft elevation model
topIssues.surface: Fasting, what's-new, paywall cards (ix-60/61/62, dl-whats-new, dl-paywall)
topIssues.why: Every card in the group uses borderWidth:1 + colors.border as its only depth (4 instances, HEAD-verified). They read flat/Generic — wireframes coloured in — against a 2026 best-in-class bar (Things/Oura/Cron) that uses one soft low-opacity drop shadow and no border. This is one root primitive, not 4 separate fixes.
topIssues.fix: Define one card elevation token (soft ~8% shadow, no border) and apply it to the shared card surface so all three screens inherit it. design-system-enforcer encodes; flag-gate the rollout.

topIssues.title: Three corner radii across four surfaces, one of them off-token
topIssues.surface: Paywall card r:18, fasting/whats-new cards r:8, error button r:12 (paywall.tsx:750, fasting.tsx:239, whats-new.tsx:110, RootErrorBoundary.tsx:94)
topIssues.why: Card corner radius is 18 vs 8 vs 12 within a single feature group, and 18 has no home in the Radius scale (which tops out at xl:12). The corner language reads as built by different hands.
topIssues.fix: Pick one card radius token (extend the scale if 16/18 is the intended card value) and route all four call-sites through it. No hardcoded radii.

topIssues.title: What's-new is the dead delight surface — a changelog advertising polish it doesn't have
topIssues.why: The screen literally lists 'haptic confirmation' and 'anticipation beats' as wins while having zero haptics and zero entrance motion itself (useLayoutEffect only, verified). It snaps in as a flat outlined list. A what's-new screen exists to make the product feel alive and improving; this one undercuts its own message.
topIssues.fix: Add a staggered fade/slide-in to the section list on appearance; mirror the same reveal on the web changelog (web-desktop-whats-new.png is also a static list). Owner: ui-product-designer, with sync-enforcer for web parity.

topIssues.title: Win-moments land via haptic+colour but have no motion celebration
topIssues.why: The two biggest emotional peaks in this group — finishing a fast and buying Pro — fire the right haptic and flip to green, but get no visual celebration before falling through to a neutral state / system Alert. At the world bar these moments earn a short motion beat (ring-fill, count-up, brief confetti) that drives the repeat/convert behaviour.
topIssues.fix: Add a one-shot completion animation: ring-fill + duration count-up on fast complete; a brief success flourish before the purchase-confirm Alert. Provide CSS analogs on web (mobile decisions apply to web). Tie each to the behaviour (habit loop / conversion).

topIssues.title: Capture harness produced 3 phantom error-boundary fall-throughs
topIssues.surface: fasting-s00.png, paywall-s00.png, whats-new-s00.png (all MD5-identical to STATE-error-boundary.png)
topIssues.why: The s00 captures for fasting/paywall/what's-new are byte-for-byte the error boundary (MD5 5548ed60…) — the harness crashed/failed to navigate and fell through. Reviewing those as the real surfaces would have produced phantom findings (the exact ENG-769 stale-bundle trap). The real surfaces were recovered from latest/ (dl-* and tour-* captures) and reviewed instead.
topIssues.fix: Hard-fail the screenshot tour when a captured frame matches the error-boundary hash, so s00 phantoms never enter a review set. Owner: executor (harness). Not a product-design defect.

reviews.signature: Cropping the logo, this would NOT reliably say "Suppr" — and worse, the logo itself doesn't say one thing. The authed product (Today / Plan / Progress / Shopping / Import) is a genuinely coherent, calm, blue-primary, soft-pastel macro-tracker with a real point of view: warm off-white canvas (#fbfaf6), one tokenised blue primary (#588CE4 / dark #7BA3EA), tinted-pastel macro bars, soft-shadow white cards, lucide glyphs, the empty-state gradient calorie ring. That product world is Premium-adjacent and on-token. But it is wrapped in a public/marketing/legal shell that drifts to TWO other accent worlds — violet (text-violet-600/400 ×99 across all trust/legal/recipe/pricing surfaces) and a pink brand-gradient (#588CE4→#DF5EBC) — AND the single most identity-load-bearing element, the brand mark, renders as three different objects depending on surface: a grey ring "plate" in-app (SupprPlateMark), a bare black "S" at auth (SupprLogoMark), and a blue-square-white-S in the design-system reference (SupprMark / dev-primitives). The signature exists inside the app; it dissolves the moment you cross into auth, pricing, or legal. The product reads as one obsessive designer's work in the authed core and a different team's work everywhere a new user first lands.
palette: ROOT — Three competing accent worlds, all in HEAD: (1) product = blue, tokenised correctly (--accent-primary #588CE4 → dark #7BA3EA); (2) marketing/legal/recipe = violet, RAW Tailwind text-violet-600/dark:text-violet-400 (×99 occurrences) in TrustPageHeader.tsx, pricing, recipe/[id], privacy/terms/dmca/licences/whats-new — NOT routed through --primary; (3) pricing = pink brand-gradient #588CE4→#DF5EBC. A user clicking from the blue authed app into Privacy/Terms sees every link flip to violet. The product primary is solid; the fix is to make the trust/legal/recipe surfaces consume --primary instead of hardcoding violet. Cite: web-desktop-roadmap (green/black), web-desktop-pricing (purple/pink), web-desktop-login (blue), web-desktop-privacy/terms (violet links).

BRAND-GRADIENT SELF-CONFLICT on Pricing (web-desktop-pricing): the declared 'fixed brand gradient' is #588CE4→#DF5EBC (PricingHero.tsx, marked 'do not replace with a token'), but the SAME page renders the Pro ribbon + CheckoutButton with a DIFFERENT gradient, from-violet-600 to-indigo-600. Two unrelated 'brand' gradients on one screen — the hero is blue→pink, the Pro card is violet→indigo. Not the gradient's existence (intentional) but the fact that pricing carries two of them.

Roadmap accent is GREEN, not blue (web-desktop-roadmap): 'Shipped' pills, status dots, and the left rail are emerald — yet the product primary is blue and pricing is pink/violet. The changelog (web-desktop-whats-new) repeats the green 'NEW' pills. Green is doing brand-accent duty on two public surfaces while meaning 'success/shipped' elsewhere — the same green now reads as both 'brand' and 'shipped/under-budget', diluting both. Decide: is green a brand accent or strictly the success token? Right now it's both.

TWO blues in the token layer: product primary #588CE4 vs the macro-protein token #6C8CFF (×4) — close enough to look like a mistake when adjacent (protein bar on Today vs the Log-a-meal button on the same screen, web-desktop-authed-today). Premium apps pick one blue family; these are two.

Colour-as-emotion gap on the win moment (web-desktop-authed-today): the empty calorie ring correctly uses the pale brand gradient (carve-out, correct). But there is no emotional escalation designed for the 'hit your target' state on web — the ring just turns success-green, the same green that already means 'roadmap shipped' and 'under budget'. The single highest-emotion moment in a tracker (closing the ring) has no dedicated warm/celebratory colour; it shares green with passive states.

NOTE — NOT a finding (phantom guard): the all-orange 'This week' and 'Daily breakdown' bars on the captured web-desktop-authed-today/progress are a STALE-CAPTURE artifact. ENG-787 (landed 08:26, after the 22:37 capture) recolours bars by effective target — amber only on over-target days (ProgressDashboard.tsx). Current code is correct per the over-budget=amber carve-out. Do not file the uniform-orange bars.
consistency: ROOT — Brand mark is THREE different components rendering THREE different objects (highest-leverage drift): SupprPlateMark (grey ring 'plate', size 32) in the in-app sidebar + today-brand-bar; SupprLogoMark (bare black 'S') on the auth/login card (web-desktop-login/signin/signup/reset-password); and SupprMark (declared 'always blue background + white S regardless of theme') on the dev-primitives reference (web-desktop-dev-primitives). The design-system page literally documents the blue-square mark as law, while no first-impression surface uses it. Worse, the token --brand-mark-bg is #fbfaf6 (near-white) with a dark ring — which matches the sidebar ring but CONTRADICTS the dev-primitives 'always blue + white S' copy. Pick one mark, one token, one component.

Wordmark colour drifts per surface: black 'Suppr' (roadmap), purple 'Suppr' (pricing), black 'S' (auth), grey-ring + black 'Suppr' (in-app sidebar). Cite web-desktop-roadmap vs web-desktop-pricing vs web-desktop-login vs web-desktop-authed-today.

Button shape consistent and good: the blue primary (#588CE4, ~rounded-xl, white text) is the same on Sign in, Create account, Log a meal, Generate meal plan, Shopping list, Import, Get the app, Back to Today (404). This is the ONE primitive carried everywhere — keep it; it's the spine of the product's cohesion. Cite web-desktop-login, web-desktop-authed-plan, web-desktop-authed-import, web-desktop-authed-settings(404).

Card elevation consistent across authed product: white cards on warm canvas with a soft low shadow, ~16px radius, on Today / Plan / Shopping / Progress. Internally cohesive — this is Good-tier and should be the documented model. Cite web-desktop-authed-today, web-desktop-authed-shopping, web-desktop-authed-plan.

Empty-state icon treatment is consistent (soft blue rounded-square tile with a lucide glyph): Plan empty calendar, Planner gateway calendar, Today 'no meals' utensil. Good cross-surface primitive. Cite web-desktop-authed-plan, web-desktop-authed-planner, web-desktop-authed-today.

Input-field styling consistent (beige/taupe filled fields, ~rounded-lg) across auth, DMCA form, and search bars — but they are notably low-contrast placeholder text on beige (you@domain.com, Your password). Same field primitive everywhere (good), but the contrast of placeholder-on-beige should be checked against 4.5:1 (forensic concern, not confirmable at this zoom). Cite web-desktop-login, web-desktop-dmca.

Documented divergences correctly present — NOT flagged: onboarding 'Join the Suppr Club' copy (web-desktop-onboarding); Planner 'lives in the iOS app' gateway (web-desktop-authed-planner) is the web/mobile move-meal carve-out; calorie-ring empty=gradient (web-desktop-authed-today).
depth: Authed product depth is Good, not flat/cheap: white cards float on the warm #fbfaf6 canvas with a soft, low-opacity shadow and consistent radius — no hairline-border-as-shadow tell in the core app. Today, Shopping, Plan, Progress all share this one elevation model. This is the strongest part of the surface group. Cite web-desktop-authed-today, web-desktop-authed-shopping.
Auth/legal cards sit at the same elevation tier (single soft-shadow white card centred on canvas) — consistent. Cite web-desktop-login, web-desktop-reset-password, web-desktop-dmca.

Depth opportunity — the calorie ring is the hero object and renders FLAT in the empty state (web-desktop-authed-today): a thin pale-lavender stroke with no inner glow, no gradient weight, no tactility. Best-in-class trackers (Oura, the macro-ring apps Suppr already beats on multi-ring concept) give the ring a sense of material — a subtle inner shadow or gradient stroke so it reads as a dial, not an SVG outline. Right now the single most-looked-at element on the home screen is the least crafted.

The 'This week' / 'Daily breakdown' bars (web-desktop-authed-today) are flat single-fill rounded bars with no depth or gradient — fine as data, but combined with the (stale) uniform colour they read wireframe-ish. Once ENG-787 colours them by target, a faint gradient or rounded-cap polish would lift them from 'charted' to 'crafted'.

Source-platform icon tiles on Import (web-desktop-authed-import) are flat soft-blue squares with generic glyphs (TikTok/Instagram/YouTube/Website) — all the same tint, no brand recognition, no depth. A premium import surface uses recognisable platform marks; these read as placeholder chips.
delight: Web has NO win-moment design at all on the surfaces captured — this is the biggest delight gap. Closing the calorie ring, hitting macro targets, completing the first-run checklist, finishing a shopping list (0/31 → 31/31) — none of these have a designed motion or colour-pulse moment in the captures. On web (no haptics) the canonical analog is a confirming micro-animation + colour pulse, and it is absent. The product's single best habit-forming moment (ring close) lands with nothing. Cite web-desktop-authed-today (ring), web-desktop-authed-shopping (0/31 progress bar — a perfect completion-celebration candidate that's currently a static hairline).

First-run success is delivered as a plain toast.success() (web-desktop-authed-today/progress/plan/shopping all show the green 'You're all set!' toast floating mid-canvas). It's a stock toast, not a designed onboarding-complete moment — and because it fires on every authed capture it visually collides with the date header and page titles. The completion of onboarding deserves a real moment (ring fill, confetti-equivalent, a warm colour wash), not a 5-second grey-green pill. NOTE: the mid-canvas position is a capture-timing artifact (toast fires on first-run), not a layout bug — but the LACK of a designed completion moment is the real finding.

No motion personality is observable across the static captures, and nothing in the surfaces suggests a shared transition language (no skeleton-to-content choreography, no number tweens on the macro counts which sit at 0/101g etc.). On web the macro numbers and the calorie total are the obvious places for a 200ms count-up tween on log — currently they appear to snap. Cite web-desktop-authed-today.

Mobile-parity reminder: any web win-moment designed here (ring-close colour pulse, count-up tween, shopping-list completion) must mirror the mobile confirm/success haptic moments per the cross-platform rule — the web motion analog and the mobile haptic should be designed as one decision, not two.

Positive: the empty/zero states are calm and on-voice ('No meals logged on this day — Tap below to search, scan, snap a photo, or use your voice') — supportive, body-neutral, no diet-shaming. The 404 (web-desktop-authed-settings) is genuinely well-handled (clean card, 'Back to Today' + 'Browse recipes'). These don't need delight added; they need the POSITIVE peaks (target hit, list done) given the same care the empty states already have.

topIssues.title: Three different brand marks across the product — grey ring (in-app), black S (auth), blue square (design-system law)
topIssues.why: The most identity-load-bearing element renders as three different objects via three components (SupprPlateMark / SupprLogoMark / SupprMark), and the --brand-mark-bg token (#fbfaf6 ring) directly contradicts the dev-primitives 'always blue + white S' declaration. A new user's first three screens (login → onboarding → Today) show three different logos. No flagship product does this.
topIssues.fix: Pick ONE canonical mark and one token. Resolve the dev-primitives contradiction (blue-square vs ring) with a decision, collapse SupprPlateMark/SupprLogoMark/SupprMark to a single component, and use it on auth, sidebar, pricing, and legal. Hand to ui-product-designer to choose the mark; design-system-enforcer to encode it as the one component + token; gate behind a flag per CLAUDE.md.

topIssues.title: Marketing/legal/recipe surfaces hardcode violet (×99) instead of the blue --primary
topIssues.surface: web-desktop-privacy, web-desktop-terms, web-desktop-dmca, web-desktop-licences, web-desktop-roadmap, web-desktop-pricing
topIssues.why: text-violet-600/dark:text-violet-400 appears 99× across TrustPageHeader and the trust/legal/recipe/pricing surfaces as raw Tailwind, never routed through --primary (#588CE4). A user crossing from the blue authed app into Privacy/Terms sees every link and accent flip to violet — the accent identity changes mid-session. Root cause is one shared component (TrustPageLayout/Header) plus pricing, not 15 pages.
topIssues.fix: Replace the raw text-violet-* utilities in TrustPageLayout/TrustPageHeader (and pricing's non-gradient violet) with the --primary token. One component change moves all six legal/trust pages. Owner: design-system-enforcer. Keep the intentional #588CE4→#DF5EBC brand gradient (PricingHero) untouched.

topIssues.title: Pricing carries two unrelated 'brand' gradients on one page
topIssues.why: The declared fixed brand gradient is blue→pink (#588CE4→#DF5EBC, PricingHero, marked do-not-tokenise), but the Pro ribbon and CheckoutButton render a different from-violet-600 to-indigo-600 gradient. The hero is blue→pink; the most-popular Pro card is violet→indigo. The emotional peak of the monetisation surface uses an accent that matches neither the brand gradient nor the product primary.
topIssues.fix: Align the Pro ribbon + CheckoutButton gradient to the canonical #588CE4→#DF5EBC brand gradient (or to --primary), removing the violet→indigo one-off. Owner: design-system-enforcer / ui-product-designer.

topIssues.title: No designed win-moment on web — ring close, target hit, list completion all land flat
topIssues.surface: web-desktop-authed-today (calorie ring + macro counts), web-desktop-authed-shopping (0/31 progress)
topIssues.why: The product's core habit loop has no celebratory motion or colour-pulse on web. Closing the calorie ring just swaps to success-green (a colour already overloaded with 'shipped' and 'under budget'); macro numbers appear to snap rather than tween; finishing a shopping list (0/31→31/31) updates a static hairline bar. The web analog of the mobile success-haptic is simply missing, so the moments that drive repeat behaviour fall flat.
topIssues.fix: Design one web win-moment system: ring-close colour pulse + 200ms count-up tween on macro/calorie numbers on log; a completion celebration on shopping-list 100%. Mirror the mobile success-haptic decision so web motion + mobile haptic are one spec. Owner: ui-product-designer; sync-enforcer for parity.

topIssues.title: Brand mark + auth wordmark render at low fidelity / bare 'S' on first-impression surfaces
topIssues.why: The auth card — the literal first screen for an MFP refugee landing on suppr.app — shows a bare black 'S' floating above a white card, with the only colour being the periwinkle Sign-in button. There's no brand world, no warmth, no recognisable mark. For the priority MFP-capture cohort, the cold-open is generic-SaaS, not 'a product people screenshot'.
topIssues.fix: Bring the canonical mark (once resolved in P0) and a hint of the brand world (the warm canvas is already there; add the resolved mark + a touch of the brand gradient or a recipe-photo motif) to the auth card. Owner: ui-product-designer.

topIssues.title: Calorie ring — the home-screen hero — renders as a flat thin stroke
topIssues.why: The most-looked-at object on the daily-use home screen is a single pale-lavender SVG outline with no material: no inner shadow, no gradient stroke weight, no dial tactility. Suppr's multi-ring concept already beats competitors conceptually; the execution of the ring itself is the least-crafted element on the screen.
topIssues.fix: Give the ring material: a subtle gradient stroke or inner shadow so it reads as a dial, and carry the 3-state colour mapping (gradient/green/red, per carve-out) onto a crafted surface rather than a hairline. Owner: ui-product-designer. (Single-surface execution detail — could also route to ui-critic, but it ties to the system-wide depth model so noted here.)

reviews.signature: PROVENANCE FIRST — this wall is mostly holes. Of 29 captures, the entire authed product (Today, Recipes, Plan, Progress, Discover, Profile, Settings, Shopping, account-billing) did NOT render: every authed-* route fell through to either the public landing page (authed-recipes/library/discover/planner/shopping/billing) or a 404 "We couldn't find that page" card (authed-today/progress/profile/settings), and home/signin/login/fasting all redirected to the sign-in screen. The authed session never carried into the Playwright run. So I can only review the public + auth + legal shell. On THAT shell, the signature is real and decent: a calm, editorial, cream-paper aesthetic (#fbfaf6 ground), heavy Inter display headings with tight tracking, soft 16-22px-radius cards on subtle elevation, a restrained green/pink ambient wash on hero surfaces. Crop the logo and the landing + legal pages would still read as one calm, trustworthy nutrition product. BUT the brand mark itself is split-brained: the 2026-05-19 decision moved the SupprMark to black-on-cream (--brand-mark-bg #fbfaf6 / --brand-mark-ring #1a1714), and auth/legal/roadmap correctly render that dark "S"; the LANDING header still hardcodes a blue rounded-square tile (.lp-mk { background: var(--primary); color:#fff }) and the dev-primitives brand-spec page still SAYS "Always blue background + white S" — so the product's own brand-reference page now contradicts the product's own shipped mark. That is the cropped-logo test failing at the logo. Tier on the reviewable shell: Good — calm and credible, a notch below the cold-open polish of MacroFactor/Cronometer's marketing because of the mark drift, three competing gradients, and a cookie strip cropping the hero CTA.
palette: BRAND-MARK COLOUR IS SPLIT ACROSS THE PRODUCT (root cause, source-verified). The 2026-05-19 mark decision = black-on-cream square (tokens --brand-mark-bg #fbfaf6 / --brand-mark-ring #1a1714 light, #0f0e12/#fff dark). web-mobile-signin/signup/reset-password/roadmap/terms/privacy all correctly render the dark 'S'. But web-mobile-landing.png renders a BLUE rounded-square 'S' tile because app/(landing)/landing.css .lp-mk hardcodes background:var(--primary), and web-mobile-dev-primitives.png's spec copy literally states 'Always blue background + white S regardless of theme.' So the same wordmark is two different colours one tap apart, and the brand-reference page documents the wrong one. Fix at the root: point .lp-mk at --brand-mark-bg/--brand-mark-ring and rewrite the dev-primitives caption — do not patch per-page.

THREE COMPETING 'PREMIUM' GRADIENTS ON ONE SURFACE (web-mobile-pricing.png + source). PricingHero declares a 'fixed brand gradient' #588CE4->#DF5EBC (blue->pink, matching the wordmark per its own comment), yet on the same page PricingTiersGrid's 'most popular' ribbon and CheckoutButton use raw Tailwind from-violet-600 to-indigo-600 (a different violet->indigo pair, hardcoded, no token). The cold-open paywall therefore shows two unrelated brand gradients. Collapse to the one declared brand pair as a token.

ONBOARDING vs PRICING HERO WASH DRIFT. web-mobile-onboarding.png uses a soft green->pink ambient wash behind 'Join the Suppr Club' with a FLAT solid-blue primary CTA, while web-mobile-pricing.png uses the saturated blue->purple->pink gradient block. Two different 'this is our brand colour moment' treatments on the two highest-intent surfaces. Pick one hero-gradient language and carry it across onboarding + pricing.

AUTH PRIMARY BUTTON READS AS DISABLED ON SIGN-UP. web-mobile-signup.png 'Create account' is rendered in pale washed-out blue (pre-checkbox disabled state) while web-mobile-signin.png 'Sign in' is full-saturation blue — a new user landing on sign-up first sees the primary CTA looking dead. The disabled state is too low-contrast against the enabled state to read as 'tick the box to enable' rather than 'broken'.

EMOTIONAL-PULL GAP: the public shell never uses warmth on a win. Every accent is the same cool blue/green; there is no warm colour reserved for an achievement or activation moment. On the marketing side this is fine, but the onboarding 'Join the club' moment and the post-signup confirmation toast (web-mobile-home capture shows the green 'You're all set!' strip on the real Today) are the only warm/positive beats and they use plain success-green — consider a single reserved celebratory accent so the activation moment feels different from a passive 'under budget' green.
consistency: LOGO/MARK TREATMENT (root): blue tile (landing) vs dark 'S' tile (auth/legal/roadmap) vs no mark at all (help, which only shows '<- Back to app'). Three header treatments. Drift, source-confirmed — collapse to the one --brand-mark token.

AUTH SHELL is internally consistent and good: signin/signup/login/reset-password share one card (rounded ~16px, soft shadow, cream ground), one 'Continue with Apple' black pill, one input style (filled #ECE9E2 fields, ~10px radius), one 'OR' divider. This is the most coherent cluster on the wall — hold it up as the bar.

LEGAL/INFO PAGES (privacy/terms/dmca/licences/help/whats-new/roadmap) share one strong template: '<- Back to app', display heading, 'Last updated April 2026' + V1.0 badge + PRINT/SAVE PDF + REVISION HISTORY row, Privacy/Terms/DMCA/Licences/Help sub-nav. Consistent — but roadmap and landing carry a top brand header while privacy/terms/help do NOT, so the legal cluster is itself split on whether it shows a logo.

BADGE/PILL LANGUAGE drifts: landing 'NEW' = blue solid rounded pill; whats-new 'LATEST' = mint-green pill and 'NEW' = lime pill; roadmap 'Shipped' = pale-green pill; pricing trust chips = grey outline pills with green shield icons. Several pill shapes/fills doing similar 'status' jobs with no shared token.

CTA SHAPE: landing 'Get started' = small pill (top-right); onboarding 'Join the club' = full-width rounded-rect; auth 'Sign in' = full-width rounded-rect; pricing 'Get started' top-right = outline 'Sign in' pill. Button radius is consistent-ish (~10-12px) but the primary-CTA SHAPE (pill vs full-width block) is decided per surface, not by one rule.

COOKIE-CONSENT STRIP overlaps the bottom of EVERY public capture (landing/pricing/onboarding/legal), cropping the hero CTA on landing ('Get started — it's free' is partially under the strip) and the BILLING toggle on pricing. Known component (ENG-633 lifts FAB above it on Today) but on cold-open marketing it covers the single most important first-impression button.
DEV-PRIMITIVES brand-spec copy is STALE and contradicts shipped tokens — not a parity carve-out, a documentation drift that will keep re-seeding the blue-mark mistake.
depth: Material on the reviewable shell is genuinely Good, not flat: the 404 card, auth card, pricing trust-chip rows, and legal cards all sit on real soft elevation (low-opacity shadow, no hairline-border-as-shadow tell) over the cream ground — this is crafted, not wireframe-coloured-in. Hold it as the depth bar for the rest of the product.

Pricing hero gradient block (web-mobile-pricing.png) has real depth and saturation and reads premium. The onboarding hero by contrast is a flat soft wash with a flat solid-blue button — slightly cheaper/flatter than the pricing hero for an equally important moment. Bring the onboarding CTA up to the same elevated/gradient treatment.

Input fields on auth (web-mobile-signin/signup) are filled flat #ECE9E2 boxes with placeholder text at borderline contrast — they read slightly 'form-template' next to the crafted cards around them. A subtle inner-shadow or 1px focus-ring token would lift them from generic-form to crafted.

CANNOT JUDGE the authed product's depth (Today rings, recipe cards, progress charts, planner) — every authed capture is a landing-fallback or 404. This is the biggest depth blind spot and must be re-captured before a real verdict.
delight: No motion or win-moment is observable on this shell because these are static public/marketing pages — but the structural absence to flag is that the cold-open hero CTA is covered by a cookie strip, which kills the one moment ('Get started — it's free') that should feel inviting and tappable on first load.

WEB HAS NO HAPTICS (correct — web). The web analog for win moments is motion/colour. The post-signup 'You're all set!' green strip visible in web-mobile-home.png is the right instinct but it's a static success-green toast with no entrance animation or reserved celebratory colour — on web this activation beat should land with a brief confirming micro-animation + a warmer celebratory accent, distinct from passive success-green, to reinforce the first-log/activation behaviour.

The pricing 'Save 37%' annual badge is the one place a colour nudge toward a conversion behaviour exists — it's a small green tag. This is fine but under-leveraged: the highest-value behaviour (choosing annual) gets the same green as everything else. A reserved emphasis colour/motion on the recommended plan would pull the eye to the conversion path.

CANNOT assess the real engagement layer — meal-log confirmation, target-hit celebration, streak/win moments — because the authed surfaces did not render. Flag as uncovered; the delight verdict on the actual product is pending a working authed capture run.

topIssues.title: Authed mobile-web product never rendered — wall is ~40% holes
topIssues.why: Every authed-* capture resolved to either the public landing page (recipes/library/discover/planner/shopping/billing) or a 404 'We couldn't find that page' card (today/progress/profile/settings); home/signin/login/fasting/account-billing all redirected to sign-in. The Playwright authed session did not carry, so the entire core product (Today rings, recipe cards, progress charts, planner, shopping, settings) is UNREVIEWED on mobile-web. Any identity/depth/delight verdict on the product proper would be inferred, not seen — exactly the silent-partial-wall failure the brief bans.
topIssues.fix: Fix the visual-audit-authed Playwright fixture so the storageState/auth cookie actually authenticates before navigating, then re-run npm run test:e2e:visual and re-capture all authed-* mobile-web routes. Until then mark the authed product UNCOVERED on this platform — do not synthesise its design from code.

topIssues.title: Brand mark is two different colours one tap apart (and the brand-spec page documents the wrong one)
topIssues.surface: web-mobile-landing.png (blue tile) vs web-mobile-signin/signup/roadmap/terms/privacy (dark 'S') vs web-mobile-dev-primitives.png (spec says 'always blue')
topIssues.why: Source-confirmed: the 2026-05-19 decision moved SupprMark to black-on-cream via --brand-mark-bg/--brand-mark-ring (suppr-mark.tsx, SupprLogoMark.tsx both migrated). Auth/legal/roadmap render the correct dark mark. But app/(landing)/landing.css .lp-mk still hardcodes background:var(--primary) (blue) + color:#fff, and the dev/primitives brand-reference page caption still reads 'Always blue background + white S regardless of theme.' So the company's own brand-spec page contradicts the company's own shipped logo, and the highest-traffic surface (landing) carries the OLD mark. Fails the cropped-logo identity test at the actual logo.
topIssues.fix: Repoint .lp-brand .lp-mk and .lp-ws-brand .lp-mk to var(--brand-mark-bg)/var(--brand-mark-ring) (or use the SupprLogoMark component), and rewrite the dev-primitives caption to the black-on-cream / white-on-black spec. One token + one copy fix moves every landing surface. Gate behind isFeatureEnabled per CLAUDE.md. Owner: design-system-enforcer (token) + executor (copy).

topIssues.title: Three competing brand gradients on the cold-open paywall
topIssues.why: PricingHero declares #588CE4->#DF5EBC the 'fixed brand gradient' (and its comment says it matches the wordmark one-to-one), yet on the same page the 'most popular' ribbon and the CheckoutButton both use raw Tailwind from-violet-600 to-indigo-600 — a different violet->indigo gradient, hardcoded, no token. The pricing page is a primary cold-open conversion surface and it shows two unrelated 'brand' gradients within one viewport, reading as two design systems.
topIssues.fix: Define ONE brand-gradient token (the declared #588CE4->#DF5EBC pair) and apply it to the hero, the popular-tier ribbon, and the primary checkout button. Delete the from-violet-600/to-indigo-600 hardcodes. Owner: design-system-enforcer.

topIssues.title: Sign-up primary CTA reads as broken/disabled on first load
topIssues.why: 'Create account' renders in pale washed-out blue (the pre-checkbox disabled state) while the adjacent flow's 'Sign in' is full-saturation blue. A new user — the priority MFP-refugee cohort — lands on sign-up first and sees the primary action looking dead, with no obvious cue that ticking the ToS box enables it. The disabled-vs-enabled delta is too subtle to read as intentional.
topIssues.fix: Make the disabled state read as clearly-waiting (e.g. neutral/ghost with a 'Tick to agree' helper) rather than a faded version of the brand blue, so the enabled transition is a visible reward. Owner: ui-product-designer.

topIssues.title: Cookie-consent strip crops the hero CTA on cold-open marketing
topIssues.why: The fixed consent strip sits at the bottom of every public capture and partially covers the single most important first-impression button — landing's 'Get started — it's free' is half under the strip, pricing's billing toggle is grazed. ENG-633 already lifts the FAB above it on Today, proving the team knows it occludes CTAs; the marketing hero has the same problem and it's the literal first thing a refugee sees.
topIssues.fix: Apply the same lift-above logic to the public hero CTA, or render the consent strip as a slimmer top-anchored or corner toast on marketing routes so it never overlaps the primary CTA. Owner: ui-product-designer + executor.

topIssues.title: Onboarding hero is flatter and uses a different brand-colour treatment than the pricing hero
topIssues.why: Onboarding 'Join the Suppr Club' uses a soft flat green->pink wash + a flat solid-blue button; pricing uses a saturated blue->purple->pink gradient block with real depth. Two of the highest-intent surfaces present the brand-colour moment two different ways, and the activation surface (onboarding) is the flatter, cheaper-looking of the two.
topIssues.fix: Unify on one hero-gradient language and bring the onboarding CTA up to the elevated/gradient treatment of pricing. Owner: ui-product-designer. (Onboarding Welcome COPY divergence stays an intentional carve-out — this is about the colour/depth treatment, not the words.)

externalBar.axis: Palette & colour-as-emotion — restraint plus emotional pull on win/streak moments
externalBar.currentBar:
The 2026 bar is "calm canvas, semantic colour, earned saturation." Three rules the best apps now share:

1. A NEUTRAL, SLIGHTLY-WARM, DESATURATED CANVAS carries ~90% of every screen. Linear's late-2025/2026 refresh explicitly moved "toward a warmer gray that still feels crisp, but less saturated," away from its old cool blue-ish hue, and collapsed theming to three variables only — base, accent, contrast (linear.app/now/behind-the-latest-design-refresh, 2026). Things 3 (v3.22, iOS 26, 2026) and Notion sit on near-white warm greys (#fafafa / #f7f6f3). The canvas is deliberately quiet so colour means something when it appears.

2. ONE ACCENT, USED SPARINGLY, IS THE PREMIUM SIGNAL. Arc has the user pick a single accent at first launch and themes the whole browser off it; Linear runs a single desaturated violet/blue. The 60-30-10 discipline holds — accent is the 10%, never the 30% (IxDF "UI Color Palette 2026"). Saturation is a scarce resource spent on the thing that matters, not sprayed across the chrome.

3. COLOUR IS SEMANTIC AND STATE-DRIVEN, NOT DECORATIVE — and this is where emotion lives. Oura's 2025/26 redesign built a "unified semantic color language" where "the new design system uses color to signal your body's different states according to your biometrics, giving you an immediate visual signal of how you're doing each day" (ouraring.com/blog/new-app-design, 2026). Copilot Money's palette "adapts to your spending patterns" — green on-track / yellow approaching / red over — making status readable "in under three seconds" (StackSwitch, SaaSweep reviews 2026). The emotional pull comes from the green/positive state being legible and slightly rewarding, not from confetti.

On WIN/STREAK moments specifically, 2026 has firmly turned AGAINST gaudy celebration. The trend consensus: "Gamification will be replaced with calmer micro-interactions... Hover states that sparkle like confetti are being replaced with animation that communicates state, structure, and system intent" (Tubik, Envato Elements 2026). Celebrations are "the product's punctuation marks that confirm, nudge, and celebrate without interrupting the sentence — timing, threshold, and context matter more than fancy trajectories." The bar for a win moment: a brief, restrained colour-and-motion beat (a fill completing, an accent/success colour blooming for ~300-600ms, a single haptic), thresholded so it only fires on a genuine milestone — never on every log. Cal AI does milestone celebrations + animated badges but reserves them for streak milestones, not routine actions (Cal AI reviews, 2026).

examples.what: 2026 refresh moved to a warmer, less-saturated grey and collapsed the theme system to three variables (base, accent, contrast). Proof that 'restraint' in 2026 means a quiet warm-neutral canvas with ONE accent — colour is reserved, not decorative. (linear.app/now/behind-the-latest-design-refresh)

2025/26 redesign introduced a 'unified semantic color language' that 'uses color to signal your body's different states' for an immediate daily read. The win/positive state is a colour cue at the at-a-glance layer, not a separate celebration screen. (ouraring.com/blog/new-app-design)

Palette 'adapts to your spending patterns'; green on-track / yellow approaching / red over makes status scannable in under three seconds — and v4.3 added colourblind-safe chart colours. Emotional pull comes from the positive (green) state being legible and calm. The prettier app got opened 1.4x/day vs YNAB 0.6x. (StackSwitch & SaaSweep reviews 2026)
Warm near-white canvases (#fafafa, #f7f6f3) — the exact references Suppr already pins its #fbfaf6 background to. Confirms Suppr's canvas is already at the 2026 bar.

User picks a single accent at first launch; the whole browser themes off it, with 'micro-delight' reserved for moments, not chrome. Model for one-accent identity + thresholded delight. (popsci, efficient.app 2026)
Milestone celebrations and animated badges, but gated to streak milestones and consistent behaviour — not every log. The calibrated version of nutrition-app gamification. (Cal AI reviews 2026)

'Gamification replaced with calmer micro-interactions'; confetti-sparkle replaced by 'animation that communicates state'; celebrations as 'punctuation marks' gated by timing/threshold/context. The explicit anti-confetti bar for win moments.

suprMoves: KEEP the canvas — don't touch it. Suppr's #fbfaf6 warm-cream base, single blue accent (#588CE4), and one-of-eight-hue Photos-anchored token system already sit exactly at the Linear/Things/Cron 2026 bar. The CSS comment even cites Notion/Things/Cron as references. This axis is a polish-the-win-moment job, not a repalette.

Define a dedicated 'win' state colour beat distinct from the static success token. Today, success-green (#56A775) does double duty as both the calories macro and the under-budget state — so a completed day looks the same as a half-logged one. For the moment a day closes on-target (or a streak milestone), let the success-soft fill (rgba(86,167,117,0.12)) bloom up to a richer success fill for ~400-500ms then settle back — the desaturated-to-saturated-to-rest beat Copilot/Oura use. Restraint preserved because it fires once, on threshold, then returns to calm.

Threshold celebrations hard — fire ONLY on genuine milestones (first log of the day, target hit, 3/7/30-day streak), never on every meal log. This is the explicit 2026 line (Tubik: celebrations are 'punctuation marks', not every word; Cal AI gates badges to streak milestones). Wire the threshold logic into the existing analytics/streak state rather than the log handler.

Pair the colour beat with ONE haptic and a sub-second spring, not confetti. The theme already references a 'spring used for sheet present + confirm-success' (theme.css line 15) and mobile has a success spring — reuse it. A single notification-success haptic + the success-colour bloom is the 2026-correct win moment; a particle burst would read as 2021.

Make the positive/under-budget state quietly legible at a glance, Copilot-style — the emotional pull is mostly the green 'you're on track' read being calm and instant, not the animation. Audit the Today ring + macro tiles so the on-track state is unmistakably the success hue and the over-budget state is unmistakably amber (#F78A32) — Suppr already has the calorie-ring 3-state mapping (gradient / success-green / destructive-red) and amber-over-budget; verify it renders with enough chroma separation to be read in under three seconds without reading numbers.

Reserve saturation for the win — keep everything else desaturated. Because the canvas and chrome are already low-saturation warm neutrals, a single saturated success bloom will carry disproportionate emotional weight (the Linear/Arc principle: scarce saturation = premium signal). Resist adding accent colour anywhere in the chrome 'to balance it' — that would dilute the one moment that should pop.

Gate the new win-moment treatment behind a feature flag per the CLAUDE.md non-negotiable (isFeatureEnabled web / mobile equivalent), keep the old path in the else, and capture before/after on web + mobile + a Storybook proof — animation timing changes and colour-meaning changes are exactly the in-scope category for flag-gated visual rollout.

externalBar.axis: Material depth & elevation (flat-vs-crafted: shadow systems, layering, tactility)
externalBar.currentBar: The 2026 bar is "purposeful depth, not decorative depth" — depth as information about the z-axis, executed with restraint. Five concrete sub-bars define best-in-class right now: (1) ELEVATION IS A TOKENISED LADDER, NOT AD-HOC. Best apps run a 4-6 tier system (base/card/dropdown/sheet/modal) where every raised surface pulls from a named token; no component invents its own shadow. (2) DARK MODE USES TONAL LIFT, NOT SHADOWS. The hard rule of 2026: black drop-shadows are invisible on dark canvases, so separation comes from luminance — each elevation step is +5-8% surface lightness (Material 3 surface-container ladder; Linear's LCH opacity-of-black/white system), reinforced by a 1px top hairline highlight to fake a lit edge. Shadows are a light-mode-only tool. (3) THE FLAT-VS-CRAFTED ANSWER IS 'FLAT SURFACES, CRAFTED EDGES + MOTION'. The winning grammar (Linear, Things, Notion) separates content surfaces with hairline borders + subtle luminance, NOT diffuse shadows; reserves real shadow for things that genuinely float (FAB, sheets, popovers, dragged items); and puts the 'tactility' into interaction physics — press-down scale (0.96-0.98), lift-on-touch, edge-distortion on overscroll — rather than into static skeuomorphic relief. (4) SHADOWS, WHEN USED, ARE SOFT + DIRECTIONAL + LAYERED. One consistent top-down light source; soft low-opacity ambient (~0.06-0.12 in light mode) rather than one hard dark drop; often two stacked shadows (tight contact + wide ambient); larger blur = higher in the z-stack. (5) GLASS IS A FUNCTIONAL LAYER WITH A LEGIBILITY FLOOR. iOS 26 Liquid Glass / Linear's disciplined take: translucency is reserved for the floating control/chrome layer that sits ABOVE content (nav bars, toolbars, sheets), always with a scrim behind text and an automatic swap to a solid high-contrast surface under Increase Contrast / Reduce Transparency. Refraction is dropped where it hurts dense reading. The meta-bar: depth must survive accessibility settings and never cost legibility — every effect has a graceful solid fallback.

examples.what: Disciplined 'ProKit' depth: keeps Apple's translucency/physicality but DROPS refraction because it 'makes dense professional interfaces harder to read.' Surface separation comes from luminance — opacities of black/white in LCH space across a base→foreground→panel→dialog→modal ladder — not drop shadows. Tactility is physics: elements lift slightly on touch, distort on overscroll, with a real-time GPU-lit highlight (SDF specular) that shifts as you scroll. Avoids strong diffuse shadows; uses tight contained ones (e.g. rgba(0,0,0,0.4) 0 2px 4px). Auto-adds solid outlines under Increase Contrast. This is the canonical 'crafted-but-flat' reference.

examples.app: Apple iOS 26 (Liquid Glass, Apple Newsroom + Developer design gallery, 2026)
examples.what: Establishes the floating-control-layer model: chrome (nav/tab/toolbar) is a distinct translucent Liquid Glass layer ABOVE content that morphs and gives way to content; the 'Solid Layer' (critical text/icons/buttons) must stay opaque and readable. Metal-4-accelerated real-time blur/specular at 60fps. Sets the system expectation users now bring to every iOS app — and the requirement to degrade to solid under Reduce Transparency.

The concrete dark-mode spec: tonal elevation via surface-container ladder (surface, -low, -high, -highest) where each step is ~+5-8% luminance / a stronger primary-tint overlay — depth WITHOUT relying on shadows. Numeric token roles (elevation-0 base → 1 card → 2 dropdown → 3 modal). This is the citable source for 'step up luminance, not shadow' in dark mode.

Widely held 'best-designed iOS finance app' — adopted the iOS 26 style in its first refresh since launch; every chart/card/animation reads as intentional, with a card-based dashboard whose visual hierarchy guides the eye. Proof that in a data-dense personal-tracking app (closest genre to Suppr), the premium signal is restraint + motion polish, not heavy material relief.

The tactility benchmark: 'one of the most tactile, fast-as-you-can-move apps around,' where depth lives in purposeful micro-interactions and animation rather than skeuomorphic shadow. Surfaces are near-flat with hairline separation; the craft is in the motion and timing. Suppr's own theme comments already cite 'Notion / Linear / Things 3 grammar' as the target.

Health-tracking peer: redesign cut clutter to surface 'one big thing' per day on a calm canvas — depth is used sparingly to elevate the single most important card, not to decorate every tile. Direct analog for how Suppr's Today hero should use elevation to rank one moment, matching the 'higher elevation = primary/urgent' rule.

examples.app: MacroFactor / Cronometer / Cal AI (named competitor set, 2026 reviews)
examples.what: The nutrition-tracker baseline Suppr must beat: MacroFactor wins on speed/data rigor with a clean flat data UI; Cal AI optimises snap-and-go; Cronometer is dense and utilitarian. None are cited for material/depth craft — depth+motion polish is an open differentiator lane for Suppr (matches the 'multi-ring + calm voice already exceeds comps' defended-choices note).

suprMoves: Tokenise the ladder and kill the drift. Suppr already has the right PHILOSOPHY in apps/mobile/constants/theme.ts (Elevation.card = hairline-only 'Notion/Linear/Things 3 grammar'; shadow reserved for FAB + sheets; cardElevated for premium tier) — but a component sweep found 12+ distinct ad-hoc shadow combos (shadowOpacity 0.06/0.08/0.12/0.15/0.18/0.22/0.45/0.6, radius 2-24) hardcoded across components. Promote a named 4-tier token set (elevation.flat → card → raised/popover → sheet → modal) and route EVERY surface through it. This is the single highest-leverage move: best-in-class = one ladder, zero one-off shadows. Mirror identical tier semantics to the web side (NutritionTracker / today-meals-section) so platforms can't drift.

Codify the dark-mode rule explicitly: shadows OFF, tonal lift ON. Suppr's dark tokens (card #1a181f → cardElevated #221f29 ≈ +8% luminance) already nail the M3 5-8%-per-step guidance — lock it in as the documented rule and extend to a full ladder (add a -low and -highest step) so dialogs/sheets/nested cards each get a distinct luminance, never a black drop-shadow. Pair every dark raised surface with a 1px top hairline highlight (the theme note already mentions layering one on the card primitive) to fake a lit edge — that's the Linear/M3 move for separation without shadow.

Move 'tactility' from static relief into interaction physics. Don't chase neumorphism/claymorphism (the 2026 verdict: a contrast/legibility trap, and off-brand for the calm voice). Instead adopt the Linear/Things grammar: press-down scale 0.96-0.98 + subtle opacity on every tappable card/row, lift-on-touch for draggable meal rows, and edge-resistance on overscroll. This buys the 'crafted/premium feel' the competitor set lacks, with zero legibility cost — and it's an open lane vs MacroFactor/Cal AI which read as flat-and-static.

Soften and standardise the light-mode shadows that remain. The FAB/sheet shadows are the only legit floaters — make them soft + directional + ideally two-layer (a tight contact shadow + a wider ambient one, single top-down light source), and cap ambient opacity around 0.06-0.12 so nothing reads as a hard 2010s drop-shadow. The current FAB accent shadow (shadowOpacity 0.45 on Accent.primary) is fine as a deliberate 'this glows' signal — keep it as the ONE chromatic shadow, document it as intentional so it doesn't read as drift.

Adopt the iOS-26 floating-chrome model only where it's a control layer, with a hard legibility floor. Suppr's LogSheet and bottom sheets are the right candidates for a translucent Liquid-Glass treatment (they overlay content) — but ONLY with a scrim behind any text and an automatic swap to a solid high-contrast surface under Reduce Transparency / Increase Contrast. Do NOT glassify content cards (macro tiles, meal rows) — that's the legibility mistake. Gate the glass treatment behind a feature flag per CLAUDE.md visual-change rule, ship behind isFeatureEnabled, validate in sim + capture before/after.

Use elevation to rank ONE moment, Oura-style. On the Today hero, let the single 'what to eat next' / primary card sit one tier higher than the surrounding tiles (luminance lift in dark, hairline+subtle shadow in light) so depth encodes priority, matching the 2026 rule 'higher elevation = primary/urgent action.' Keep the 2x2 macro tiles deliberately flat so the hero reads as the one elevated thing — reinforces the existing 'don't duplicate Today hero content' direction.

externalBar.axis: Motion & transitions — motion personality, meaningful transitions, number tweens, sheet choreography
externalBar.currentBar: In 2026 the bar is "physics, not duration." Best-in-class apps abandoned fixed ease curves for spring systems with a deliberate, consistent personality, and every animation either communicates spatial origin or confirms an action — decorative motion now reads as amateur. Six concrete sub-bars: (1) MOTION PERSONALITY — one tuned spring family used app-wide, not per-screen one-offs. Apple's iOS 26 "Liquid Glass" is the reference: elements morph and flow with "fluid tension — deliberate, not exaggerated," and iOS 26.2 (early 2026) explicitly re-tuned system springs smoother. SwiftUI's 2026 default vocabulary: .snappy (~0.25s, response 0.3 / damping ~0.85) for taps/toggles, .smooth (~0.35s, ~response 0.32 / damping 0.72, "feels like Apple Music") for content, .bouncy reserved for celebratory moments only. The discipline is picking ONE and applying it everywhere. (2) SPEED & RESTRAINT — Linear's 2026 refresh (Mar 12 2026) is the craft benchmark: animate only GPU-composited properties (transform/opacity, sometimes color), never layout; durations below industry norm (quick 0.1s, regular 0.25s, slow 0.35s); list items get NO transition so scrolling stays instant; timing is asymmetric (appear 0s, fade out 0.15s, mirroring macOS). Speed itself is the personality. (3) MEANINGFUL/SPATIAL TRANSITIONS — motion references its origin. Linear scales popovers from the pill that spawned them, slides panels from their toggle. iOS 18+/26 made this first-class: zoom transitions (matchedTransitionSource + navigationTransition) and matchedGeometryEffect morph a tapped cell continuously into the detail it opens, across pushes AND sheets. A cross-fade where a morph is possible now reads cheap. (4) NUMBER TWEENS — the single most-copied 2026 detail. NumberFlow (6.3k stars, used by X and Dub, explicitly inspired by the Family iOS app) made digit-by-digit odometer rolls — each digit spins/fades/transforms independently, Intl.NumberFormat-aware — the default for any stat/balance/counter that changes. Static numbers that snap feel broken when surrounding craft is high. (5) SHEET CHOREOGRAPHY — sheets are interruptible spring-driven objects, not timed slides: native detents (iOS 16+) with custom heights, drag tracks the finger 1:1, snap decided by velocity+position, open animation redirectable mid-flight (interactive spring ~response 0.35 / damping 0.8). (6) HAPTIC-MOTION FUSION — 2026 users expect a synchronized haptic at the motion's key moment (slider "stick," completed-swipe "thrum," success on a confirmed log). Motion without a paired haptic on consequential actions reads incomplete. All of it must collapse cleanly to cross-fades under Reduce Motion — iOS 26.4 (Apr 2026) added finer Liquid Glass motion controls precisely because honoring accessibility is table stakes.

examples.what: System-wide elastic morphing material: UI elements glide/morph beneath curved glass with 'fluid tension, deliberate not exaggerated.' iOS 26.2 re-tuned springs smoother for app-launch/page-slide/menu; iOS 26.4 (Apr 2026) added finer Reduce-Motion controls. Reference for one coherent motion personality across an OS, with accessibility fallbacks built in.

Speed/restraint benchmark. Animates only composited props (transform/opacity/color), never layout; tokens quick 0.1s / regular 0.25s / slow 0.35s, below norm; list items get zero transition; asymmetric timing (fade-in 0s, fade-out 0.15s); popovers scale from origin pill, panels slide from toggle. Speed is the personality.

Family's number animations inspired NumberFlow, the digit-roll component (6.3k stars; used by X and Dub). Sets the value-tween bar: per-digit odometer roll with spin/fade/transform, Intl.NumberFormat-aware, 'Apple-like smoothness.' A React Native port (number-flow-react-native, Skia+View renderers) exists — directly reachable on Suppr's stack.

matchedTransitionSource + navigationTransition(.zoom) and matchedGeometryEffect: a tapped cell continuously morphs into the detail view it opens, across navigation pushes AND sheet presentations. Current premium standard for meaningful, spatially-grounded transitions — replaces generic push/cross-fade.

Credited with resetting 'what a budget app could look like' via smooth native animations, clean typography, native iOS components. Closest-domain (number-heavy finance dashboard) example of motion as a premium signal — relevant because Suppr's Today surface is structurally a number dashboard.

Health-data peers. Oura's redesign surfaces the one metric 'your body most needs now' with score-reveal motion + charts; the 2026 wearable shift is from raw data-viz to motion-supported guidance. Calibrates Suppr's ring/score reveals against the closest behavioural-data category.

examples.app: Cal AI vs MacroFactor (2026, direct competitors)
examples.what: Cal AI optimises the log loop to 'under five seconds' (photo to estimate) — motion must never sit in that critical path. MacroFactor optimises rigor + adaptive coaching. Competitor read: Suppr's log confirmation must feel instant and rewarding (snappy + success haptic), differentiating on a calmer, more confident confirmation.

Craft references outside the data domain. Things' rebuild praised for 'craft most task managers never approach' in its animations; Notion Calendar 'obsessed over micro-interactions' in a category that ships none. Both show motion as category-differentiator, the posture Suppr should take in calorie tracking.

suprMoves: Adopt ONE spring vocabulary app-wide and delete per-screen one-offs. Suppr has the right primitives but they disagree: CalorieRing ring uses spring damping 22 / stiffness 120, its number tween a hand-rolled 400ms cubic-out, PressableScale 0.97/100ms + spring release, macro bars 300ms cubic-out. Define a small motion-token module (snappy ~response 0.3/damping 0.85 for taps/toggles; smooth ~0.32/0.72 for content/bars; bouncy for celebratory only) and route every withSpring/withTiming through it. This is the Linear/iOS-26 'single personality' move, mostly a refactor of motion that already exists.

Make number tweens odometer-style, not snap-to-value — highest-leverage, most-visible 2026 detail and Suppr's biggest gap. Today's calories-remaining, net, and the four macro grams should roll digit-by-digit (NumberFlow / number-flow-react-native, Skia or View renderer, Reanimated-compatible) instead of the current single-value cubic-out interpolation. Family/Copilot Money are the calibration: Suppr's calmer brand means slightly slower, less-bouncy rolls than a finance ticker — confident, not flashy.

Replace the LogSheet open with a meaningful spatial transition. The meal / quick-add element the user taps should morph into the sheet (iOS-26 zoom / matchedTransitionSource on native; FLIP / layoutId on web) instead of sliding up generically, so the thing touched becomes the sheet. Gate behind a feature flag per CLAUDE.md (keep the current slide in the else), ramp via PostHog. This is the origin-referenced-motion bar from Linear and iOS 26.

Make every sheet (LogSheet, SavedMealPortionSheet, AiPaywallSheet, MoveMealSheet) interruptible and velocity-aware: detents with 1:1 finger tracking, snap decided by velocity+position (not position alone), open spring redirectable mid-flight to dismiss (interactive spring ~response 0.35 / damping 0.8). Audit Suppr's sheets for timed slides that can't be grabbed mid-animation.

Pair a haptic with every consequential motion, not just press-in. PressableScale already fires selection/confirm/success on press — extend so the haptic lands at the motion's payoff: success haptic the moment a logged meal lands and the calorie number finishes its roll; a light tick as a portion stepper / ruler slider 'sticks' to a value; a confirm thrum when a sheet snaps to a detent. This is 2026 haptic-motion fusion and Suppr is halfway there.

Borrow Linear's restraint explicitly. Audit for animations on layout props (width/height/margin) and move them to transform/opacity; ensure TodayMealsSection list rows have NO per-item entry transition (keep scroll instant); adopt asymmetric timing (content appears fast ~0–0.1s, fades out slower ~0.15s). Suppr's 300ms macro bars are near-norm — pull the system toward Linear's quicker 0.1/0.25/0.35 band so the whole app reads faster.

Make the calorie-ring + score reveal a deliberate once-per-load choreography (Oura-style) instead of continuous re-animation. Ring sweep, number roll, and macro bars should stagger ~40–80ms on first paint of the day's data so the reveal feels composed (priority/ordering), then settle — not re-tween on every minor re-render. Reserve the bouncy spring exclusively for hitting a goal (ring closes / target reached), so bounce means something.

Verify the whole motion system collapses to cross-fades under Reduce Motion before shipping. CalorieRing already reads reduceMotion — make that a system-wide guarantee (every spring to instant/cross-fade, every odometer roll to instant set) and capture a Reduce-Motion pass in the visual-validation screenshots, since iOS 26.4 raised expectations here and Suppr's premium-bar audits require captured pixels.

externalBar.axis: Micro-delight & haptic-equivalent feedback (iOS haptics + the web motion/colour analog; making logging and win-moments feel rewarding)
externalBar.currentBar:
In 2026 the bar is a TIERED feedback system, not "vibrate on tap." Best-in-class apps spend a deliberate vocabulary of haptics + motion that scales with event importance, and they ruthlessly RESERVE the loud moments. The concrete shape:

1) A graded haptic vocabulary mapped to event weight. iOS gives three impact intensities (light/medium/heavy via UIImpactFeedbackGenerator), a selection tick (selectionAsync), and three semantic notifications (success/warning/error). The 2026 expectation (per Apple HIG "Playing haptics" and the May-2026 micro-interaction rules) is that intensity tracks importance: a row-select gets a light selection tick; committing a log gets a success notification; a destructive delete gets a heavier hit. Telegram is the canonical reference — every gesture (swipe-reply, swipe-read, long-press-react) has a DISTINCT haptic so the thumb knows what fired before the eyes confirm. SwiftUI codified this with declarative .sensoryFeedback(). The anti-pattern, explicitly called out by NN/g and Dan Saffer ("microinteractions are an exercise in restraint") in 2026 writeups, is firing the SAME haptic on everything — that produces fatigue and the signal goes to noise.

2) Sub-perceptual latency + duration budgets. The May-2026 micro-interaction rules put hard numbers on it: visible feedback must land within ~100ms of the action (or it reads as broken), and a CELEBRATORY flourish must finish under ~300ms (or it becomes an obstacle to the next action). Cal AI's log-confirm card eases in over ~200ms as a "deliberate moment, not a pop-in" — Suppr already cites this internally on NorthStarBlock (220ms fade-up). That timing band is the table-stakes envelope.

3) A reserved "win moment" — landmark-gated, not per-action. This is the single biggest 2026 differentiator and the one Suppr is missing. The leaders explicitly DON'T celebrate every action with the same animation; they save the loud moment for landmarks, and the data backs it: Duolingo's redesigned streak/phoenix celebration alone moved day-7 retention +1.7%, and they reserve full custom reveals for landmark days (7/30/100/365), pairing visual + functional + social rewards with confetti + a distinct sound + success haptic. MacroFactor (May 2026) goes further — hitting your nutrition goal launches an actual interactive mini-game ("clear the meteor storm, stay in orbit"), treating goal-completion as a moment worth interrupting for. The principle: ordinary logging = quiet tick; closing the day / hitting target / streak landmark = the ONE loud, multi-sensory moment.

4) The web motion/colour analog is a real, owned layer — not "haptics minus the buzz." Web has no Taptic Engine, so 2026 leaders substitute: a snappy spring/scale confirm (GPU transform only — Motion.dev/Framer useSpring), a colour-state flip (the calorie ring going success-green, a row flashing then settling), and canvas-confetti for landmark wins (<100 particles for 60fps).
